title: Compare Integers Using Number Line Order
description: Determine which of two integers is greater based on their positions on the number line.
condition: Two integers (positive or negative) are given for comparison.
response: State the correct inequality (< or >) based on which number lies to the right on the number line.
label: Principle
example: Compare $-8$ and $-13$. Since $-8$ is to the right of $-13$, $-8 > -13$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compare Negative Numbers
description: When comparing two negative numbers, recognize that the number closer to zero is greater.
condition: Given two negative numbers.
response: Determine which number is greater by comparing their distances from zero.
label: Principle / Model
example: Compare $-11$ and $-22$. Since $-11$ is closer to 0, $-11 > -22$.

title: Determine the Least Common Multiple (LCM)
description: Given two whole numbers, determine the smallest positive number that is a multiple of both numbers.
condition: Two whole numbers are given and a smallest shared total (common multiple) is required.
response: Identify common multiples of both numbers and select the least positive one (the LCM).
label: Principle
example:
For 12 and 9:
Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, 48, ...
Multiples of 9: 9, 18, 27, 36, ...
Common multiples: 36, 72, ...
Least common multiple: $\mathrm{LCM}(12,9)=36$.
notes: Selecting the least common multiple justifies equal totals with minimal quantity.

title: Distance as Absolute Difference
description: Find the distance between two numbers on a number line by taking the absolute value of their difference.
condition: Two numerical coordinates along the same axis are given (same x or same y).
response: Compute the distance as |a - b|.
label: Principle
example: Distance between y = 4 and y = -4 is $|4 - (-4)| = |8| = 8$.
notes: Used after identifying that points share one coordinate.

title: Distance on Horizontal or Vertical Line
description: Determine the length of a horizontal or vertical segment by comparing the differing coordinate only.
condition: Two points share the same x-coordinate or the same y-coordinate.
response: Subtract the differing coordinates and take the absolute value to find the segment length.
label: Principle
example: For C(-5, -1) and D(-7, -1), distance is $|-5 - (-7)| = 2$ units.

title: Evaluate Absolute Value
description: Compute the absolute value of a number as its distance from zero on the number line.
condition: Given an expression of the form $|a|$.
response: State the nonnegative distance of $a$ from zero.
label: Principle / Model
example: $|-8| = 8$ because the distance from 0 to $-8$ is 8 units.

title: Find Missing Rectangle Vertex
description: Determine the fourth vertex of an axis-aligned rectangle using matching x- and y-coordinates.
condition: Three vertices of a rectangle are given with sides parallel to axes.
response: Combine the unmatched x-coordinate with the unmatched y-coordinate to form the missing point.
label: Principle
example: Given R(-7,10), E(-7,-10), and C(7,10), the missing vertex is $(7,-10)$.

title: Find Numbers with Given Absolute Value
description: Identify all numbers whose absolute value equals a specified positive number.
condition: Given an equation of the form $|x| = a$, where $a > 0$.
response: State both solutions $x = a$ and $x = -a$.
label: Principle / Model

title: Find the Opposite of a Number
description: Determine the opposite (additive inverse) of a given number.
condition: Given a rational number $a$.
response: State $-a$.
label: Principle / Model
example: The opposite of $-8$ is $8$ because $-8 + 8 = 0$.

title: Form Equal Groups Using a Common Factor
description: Use a common factor of two quantities to divide both into the same number of equal groups.
condition: Two totals are given and groups must be identical with each group containing both quantities.
response: Choose a common factor of both totals as the number of groups and divide each total by that factor to find the amount per group.
label: Rule
example:
30 tulips and 40 daffodils. Choose 5 groups (since 5 is a common factor of 30 and 40).
Each group has $30\div5=6$ tulips and $40\div5=8$ daffodils.
notes: Any common factor can be used to create valid equal groups.

title: Graph a Strict Inequality on a Number Line
description: Represent all solutions to a strict inequality using an open circle and shading in the correct direction.
condition: Given a strict inequality in one variable (e.g., t < 10 or n > -3).
response: Place an open circle at the boundary value and shade the number line in the direction of all values that satisfy the inequality.
label: Production / Skill
example: For $t < 10$, draw an open circle at $10$ and shade to the left.
notes: Direction of shading depends on inequality symbol.

title: Identify Common Factors of Two Numbers
description: Determine which numbers divide evenly into both given numbers.
condition: Two whole numbers are given and shared divisors are requested or needed.
response: List factors of each number and identify the numbers that appear in both lists.
label: Rule
notes: Supports determining possible numbers of identical groups.

title: Identify Common Multiples of Two Numbers
description: Determine which numbers appear in the lists of multiples of both given numbers.
condition: Two whole numbers are given and a shared multiple is needed.
response: List multiples of each number and identify overlapping values.
label: Rule
example:
Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, 48, ...
Multiples of 9: 9, 18, 27, 36, 45, ...
Common multiples: 36, 72, ...
notes: Used to ensure equal totals when quantities come in fixed package sizes.

title: Identify Lowest Signed Value in Context
description: Determine the lowest elevation by identifying the smallest (most negative) rational number in context.
condition: Given several elevations represented as signed numbers.
response: Select the time or situation corresponding to the smallest numerical value.
label: Principle / Model
example: Morning: $-7$, Lunch: $11$, Afternoon: $-4$. Since $-7$ is smallest, morning is lowest.

title: Interpret Absolute Value
description: Evaluate or interpret absolute value as distance from zero on the number line.
condition: An expression of the form $|a|$ or a statement involving absolute value.
response: State the nonnegative distance of $a$ from 0.
label: Concept
example: $|-6| = 6$ because it is 6 units from 0.

title: Interpret Strict Inequality Endpoints
description: Recognize that strict inequalities (< or >) exclude the boundary value from the solution set.
condition: Given a strict inequality symbol (< or >).
response: Represent the boundary point with an open circle (not included in the solution set).
label: Category
example: For $n > -3$, the point $-3$ is shown with an open circle because $-3$ is not included.
notes: Contrast with ≤ or ≥, which use closed circles.

title: Interpret Zero in Context
description: Interpret what a value of 0 represents in a real-world signed-number context.
condition: Given a contextual situation involving positive and negative quantities relative to a reference point.
response: State that 0 represents the reference level.
label: Concept
example: If elevations are measured relative to sea level, $0$ represents sea level.

title: List Factors of a Number
description: Determine all whole numbers that divide a given whole number without remainder.
condition: A single whole number is given and its factors are requested or needed.
response: List all positive integers that divide the number evenly.
label: Rule
example: Factors of 30 are $1,2,3,5,6,10,15,30$ because each divides 30 with no remainder.
notes: Foundational step before identifying common factors.

title: List Multiples of a Number
description: Generate successive products of a whole number and counting numbers.
condition: A whole number is given and its multiples are requested or needed.
response: Multiply the number by 1, 2, 3, ... to generate its multiples.
label: Rule
example: Multiples of 9 are $9\times1=9$, $9\times2=18$, $9\times3=27$, $9\times4=36$, ...
notes: Supports identifying common multiples and the LCM.

title: Order Negative and Positive Fractions
description: Order a set of positive and negative fractions from least to greatest using their values.
condition: A list of positive and negative fractions is given.
response: Arrange the fractions from least (most negative) to greatest.
label: Principle
example: Order $-\frac{4}{5}, -\frac{3}{4}, \frac{1}{8}, \frac{1}{2}$. Since $-\frac{4}{5} < -\frac{3}{4} < \frac{1}{8} < \frac{1}{2}$, this is the correct order.

title: Plot a Point on the Coordinate Plane
description: Place a point at the location specified by an ordered pair (x, y).
condition: An ordered pair $(x,y)$ is given.
response: Mark the point x units horizontally and y units vertically from the origin.
label: Production / Skill
example: Plot $(1,-2)$ by moving 1 unit right and 2 units down from the origin.

title: Plot a Rational Number on a Number Line
description: Locate and label a rational number at its correct position on a number line.
condition: Given a rational number and a scaled number line.
response: Place a point at the corresponding location and label it with the number.
label: Production / Skill
example: To plot $-6.5$, locate the point halfway between $-6$ and $-7$ on the number line and label it $-6.5$.

title: Reflection Changes Coordinate Signs
description: Recognize that reflected points across an axis differ only by the sign of one coordinate.
condition: Two points differ only by the sign of one coordinate.
response: Identify them as reflections across the corresponding axis.
label: Concept
example: (-7, 10) and (-7, -10) are reflections across the x-axis.

title: Test Whether a Value Satisfies an Inequality
description: Determine if a given numerical value makes an inequality statement true.
condition: Given an inequality with a variable (e.g., n > -3) and a specific number to test.
response: Substitute or compare the given number to the inequality and decide whether the statement is true or false.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given $n > -3$, test $n = -2.9$. Since $-2.9 > -3$ is true, $-2.9$ is a solution.
notes: Relies on properties of the number line and order of real numbers.

title: Translate 'Less Than' into an Inequality
description: Convert a verbal statement describing a quantity being less than a number into a symbolic inequality.
condition: A verbal statement of the form 'a quantity is less than a given number.'
response: Write a symbolic inequality using < with the variable representing the quantity (e.g., t < 10).
label: Rule / Plan
example: “The temperature is less than 10 degrees” → Let $t$ be temperature: $t < 10$.

title: Write Inequality from Context
description: Translate a verbal description of a boundary or range into an inequality.
condition: A real-world description involving quantities greater than or less than a boundary value.
response: Write a correct inequality using <, >, ≤, or ≥.
label: Principle
example: If a shirt fits children up to 55 lb, a child who outgrew it satisfies $w > 55$.
